reasoning: Analysis of the major AI training copyright and data lawsuits reveals that nearly all significant cases will not reach trial by the end of 2025. Specifically, the trial for Andersen v. Stability AI is scheduled for September 2026; Authors Guild v. OpenAI is only reaching the class certification briefing stage in early 2025 (meaning a trial is likely in 2026); and New York Times v. OpenAI still has no trial date set as of March 2025. The Thomson Reuters v. ROSS Intelligence trial is scheduled for May 2025, but ROSS Intelligence is a defunct startup, making it highly unlikely that Thomson Reuters will win and be awarded a $100M+ judgment. Furthermore, while large out-of-court settlements are possible among major players, private settlements do not result in a court *ordering* a company to pay (unless it is a class-action settlement). Since no large class action classes have been certified yet, formulating a settlement, providing notice, holding fairness hearings, and receiving a final court order before December 31, 2025 is extremely improbable given the tight timeline. Lastly, there are no concrete signals of a US regulatory agency preparing a $100M+ fine explicitly for "how they used data to train" a model. Because the timeline for final judgments or approved class settlements stretches well past 2025, the probability of a YES resolution is very low.
